skill.name: diagnose
skill.description: Disciplined diagnosis loop for hard bugs and performance regressions. Reproduce → minimise → hypothesise → instrument → fix → regression-test. Use when user says "diagnose this" / "debug this", reports a bug, says something is broken/throwing/failing, or describes a performance regression.
skill.summary: Structured six-phase debugging methodology for complex issues and performance regressions.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: All 12 static analysis findings are false positives. The skill is a documentation/guide describing debugging methodology with no actual code execution. The hitl-loop.template.sh is a passive user instruction template with no real network calls or shell execution. No security concerns identified.

medium_findings.title: False Positive: External Commands Reference
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 'backtick execution' at multiple lines in SKILL.md. These are Markdown code formatting (e.g., `[DEBUG-a4f2]`, `performance.now()`, `grep`) used in documentation to describe debugging techniques. No actual shell or Ruby execution occurs. All seven flagged locations are documentation text, not executable code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Inspected all seven flagged locations - all are Markdown code formatting in documentation, not actual shell command invocations. The 'backticks' are inline code fences in the markdown document.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Hardcoded URL
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 'http://localhost:3000' at line 31 in hitl-loop.template.sh. This is a human-readable instruction in a template file that users copy and customize. No actual network request is made. The URL refers to localhost, not an external server.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is inside a step() function call that displays instructions to users, not an actual curl or fetch call. No network code is executed.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 'capture' command at line 33 in hitl-loop.template.sh. This is a user interaction function that asks human users for feedback. The 'reconnaissance' classification is incorrect - this is intentional human-in-the-loop interaction design.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The capture() function is a simple bash read that prompts a human user for input. This is documented human-in-the-loop debugging, not system reconnaissance.

content.user_title: Debug Complex Issues with a Structured Diagnosis Loop
content.value_statement: When bugs resist diagnosis or performance mysteriously degrades, use a disciplined six-phase loop: reproduce, minimise, hypothesise, instrument, fix, and regression-test. This skill provides the exact methodology to avoid guesswork and solve hard problems systematically.
actual_capabilities: Build deterministic feedback loops for any bug or performance regression
Reproduce issues with high confidence before attempting fixes
Generate and rank multiple falsifiable hypotheses to avoid anchoring
Instrument code with targeted probes that map to specific predictions
Write regression tests at the correct seam before applying fixes
Clean up debugging artifacts and document findings for future developers
limitations: Cannot build feedback loops for issues where no reproducible environment exists
Performance debugging requires baseline measurements - cannot work without them
Non-deterministic bugs with reproduction rates below 1% may remain undebuggable
Cannot help if codebase architecture prevents writing meaningful regression tests

use_cases.title: Debugging Intermittent Failures
use_cases.description: A flaky test or race condition that only fails 20% of the time. Use the methodology to raise the reproduction rate through stress testing and parallelisation until the bug is debuggable.
use_cases.target_user: QA engineers and developers investigating non-deterministic failures

use_cases.title: Performance Regression Investigation
use_cases.description: A feature that worked fast six months ago is now slow. Build a timing harness, establish a baseline, then bisect to find which change introduced the regression.
use_cases.target_user: Developers and performance engineers tracing latency increases

use_cases.title: Root Cause Analysis for Production Bugs
use_cases.description: A production issue with no clear cause. Capture a HAR file or log dump, build a minimised repro from real data, then apply the hypothesis-test loop to find the root cause.
use_cases.target_user: SRE engineers and backend developers debugging live issues

prompt_templates.title: Basic Bug Diagnosis
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the diagnose skill. My application is throwing an error: [describe error]. The bug appears when [describe trigger]. I want you to build a feedback loop and work through the diagnosis phases.
prompt_templates.scenario: Simple bug report with clear symptoms

prompt_templates.title: Performance Investigation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the diagnose skill for a performance regression. The [feature name] endpoint has gotten slower over the last [timeframe]. Current response time is [X]ms. Help me build a timing harness and find the root cause.

prompt_templates.title: Non-deterministic Failure
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the diagnose skill. This test fails randomly about [X]% of the time. I cannot reproduce it locally. Help me increase the reproduction rate and identify the root cause.

prompt_templates.title: Production Issue Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the diagnose skill. We have a production issue: [describe]. I have captured [logs/HAR file/core dump]. Help me replay the trace, build a minimised repro, and apply the diagnosis loop.

output_examples.input: This endpoint started timing out randomly. Can you debug it?
output_examples.output:
Building feedback loop... Created timing harness targeting the endpoint with 50 warmup + 100 measured requests. Baseline established: median 45ms, p99 120ms. 

Reproducing the timeout... Confirmed intermittent timeouts affecting ~15% of requests, error: 'Connection pool exhausted'. 

Generating hypotheses ranked by plausibility:
1. Connection pool size insufficient under concurrent load (probability: high)
2. Connection leak from failed cleanup (probability: medium)
3. Load balancer timeout misconfiguration (probability: low)

Instrumenting with targeted probes... Adding connection pool metrics every 5 seconds. Test running. 

[Proceed through remaining phases until root cause confirmed]

output_examples.input: Diagnose this: our image upload broke after the recent deployment
output_examples.output:
Feedback loop built: curl script targeting the upload endpoint with a 1MB test fixture, asserting on 200 response and verifying file exists in storage. 

Reproduced: upload returns 500 after deployment, 200 before. Error: 'S3 bucket permissions denied'.

Hypotheses ranked:
1. IAM role changed in deployment (probability: high)
2. Bucket name changed (probability: medium)
3. Region misconfiguration (probability: low)

Probing IAM configuration... Confirmed role was rotated, new role missing s3:PutObject permission. 

Fix applied: restored s3:PutObject policy. Regression test passes. Cleanup complete.

best_practices: Spend disproportionate time building the feedback loop - a 2-second deterministic loop beats a 30-second flaky one every time
Always generate 3-5 ranked hypotheses before testing any of them to avoid anchoring on the first plausible idea
Tag all debug instrumentation with unique prefixes like [DEBUG-a4f2] so cleanup becomes a single grep operation
anti_patterns: Proceeding to hypothesis generation without a working feedback loop - this leads to guesswork, not debugging
Testing multiple hypotheses simultaneously instead of changing one variable at a time
Skipping the regression test step when no correct seam exists - document the architecture gap instead

faq.question: What do I do if I cannot build a feedback loop for my bug?
faq.answer: Stop and explicitly tell the user you cannot proceed. List the loop-building techniques you tried. Ask for access to a reproducing environment, captured artifacts like HAR files or core dumps, or permission to add temporary instrumentation.

faq.question: How do I handle bugs with extremely low reproduction rates?
faq.answer: The goal is a higher reproduction rate, not a perfect repro. Loop the trigger 100 times, parallelise tests, add stress, narrow timing windows, inject sleeps. Keep raising the rate until it hits 50%+ where it becomes debuggable.

faq.question: When should I use the HITL bash script template?
faq.answer: Only as a last resort when no automated feedback loop is possible because a human must click through a UI. The hitl-loop.template.sh keeps the loop structured even when a person drives it, and captured output feeds back into the diagnosis.

faq.question: How do I choose where to write the regression test?
faq.answer: Find a correct seam where the test exercises the real bug pattern as it occurs at the call site. A shallow seam like a single-caller unit test gives false confidence. If no correct seam exists, document that finding - the architecture is preventing the bug from being locked down.

faq.question: What should I do after fixing the bug?
faq.answer: Re-run the Phase 1 feedback loop against the original scenario, confirm all [DEBUG-...] instrumentation is removed, delete throwaway prototypes, and state the correct hypothesis in the commit message so the next debugger learns.

faq.question: How does this skill handle non-deterministic timing issues?
faq.answer: For timing-dependent bugs, establish a baseline measurement first using timing harnesses, performance.now(), profilers, or query plans. Then bisect with that baseline rather than relying on logs, which are often wrong for performance regressions.
